coredata.dc:title: Self-exothermic redox reaction-driven green synthesis of fluorescent poly(dopamine) nanoparticles for rapid and visual detection of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanhua
Qiu, Yan
Zhou, Kai
Zhang, Kailian
Wang, Lujun
Zeng, Jing
Ji, Baian
Gao, Die
Xia, Zhining
Fu, Qifeng
coredata.dc:description: The development of fluorescent poly(dopamine) (PDA) nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention in the last few years, owing to their excellent physicochemical properties and good biocompatibility compared with fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles. Although great advances have been made to synthesize the fluorescent PDA nanoparticles (PDANPs), the development of a convenient and effective method for the large-scale synthesis of highly fluorescent PDANPs is still highly desirable. Herein, we first report a rapid, facile and green strategy for the large-scale synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs at room temperature via self-exothermic redox reaction. After a series of characterizations, it was established that the large amount of reaction heat generated by the redox reaction system composed of sodium periodate (SP) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) could effectively accelerate the oxidation and polymerization rates of dopamine (DA), leading to the rapidly spontaneous formation of PDANPs within 1 min. The PDANPs prepared under the optimum conditions showed intense fluorescence (quantum yield, 6.3%) greater than that of previously reported fluorescent PDANPs, high production yield (about 21.0%), excellent water dispersibility and good photostability. Moreover, it was found that the fluorescence of PDANPs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+. After optimizing the sensing conditions, this method shows a wide linear ranged from 0.3 to 100 μM, while the detection limit is 0.1 μM. Furthermore, this fluorescent probe was successfully utilized for detecting Fe3+ in environmental water. Ultimately, the fluorescent PDANPs were further used for the preparation of fluorescent paper sensor to visually and semi-quantitatively detect Fe3+. Thus, the prepared PDANPs show great application potential for detecting Fe3+ in the fields of sensing and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent poly(dopamine) nanoparticles
Rapid synthesis
Self-exothermic reaction
Paper sensor
Visual detection
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification of fluorescent PDANPs
Detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples
Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of Fe3+
Characterization of the as-prepared fluorescent PDANPs
Optical properties and photostability of fluorescent PDANPs
Fluorescence response of PDANPs to Fe3+
Possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to PDANPs
Detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples and visual detection of Fe3+ by paper-based sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The temperature changes of the mixed solutions composed of the reductants (NaBH4, NaHS, AA, NaHSO3 and SC) and SP within 2 min.
(A) TEM images of the as-prepared PDANPs; (inset: size distribution of PDANPs measured by TEM) and (B) FTIR spectra of DA (curve a) and the obtained PDANPs (curve b).
(A) XPS spectrum of the obtained PDANPs; (B–D) high-resolution C 1s, O 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of PDANPs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (inset at the left: photograph taken under visible light) and optimal excitation/emission fluorescence spectrum (inset at the right: photograph taken under 365 nm UV light) of PDANPs solution; (B) fluorescence spectra of PDANPs aqueous solution upon excitation at different wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of PDANPs before and after the addition of various metal ions (300 μM) in the presence and absence of 2.5 mM EDTA. The error bars represent the standard deviations based on three independent measurements; (B) Plot for fluorescent quenching value ΔF versus Fe3+ concentration (the inset shows the linear relationship between ΔF and Fe3+ concentration within the concentration range of 0.3–100 μM).
(A) Photographs of the PDANPs-doped filter papers under various metal ion conditions; (B) Photographs of the PDANPs-doped filter papers under various concentration of Fe3+.
Schematic of the preparation procedure of fluorescent PDANPs.

head.ce:title: Self-exothermic redox reaction-driven green synthesis of fluorescent poly(dopamine) nanoparticles for rapid and visual detection of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Testing Center, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical and Testing Center, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of fluorescent poly(dopamine) (PDA) nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention in the last few years, owing to their excellent physicochemical properties and good biocompatibility compared with fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles. Although great advances have been made to synthesize the fluorescent PDA nanoparticles (PDANPs), the development of a convenient and effective method for the large-scale synthesis of highly fluorescent PDANPs is still highly desirable. Herein, we first report a rapid, facile and green strategy for the large-scale synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs at room temperature via self-exothermic redox reaction. After a series of characterizations, it was established that the large amount of reaction heat generated by the redox reaction system composed of sodium periodate (SP) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) could effectively accelerate the oxidation and polymerization rates of dopamine (DA), leading to the rapidly spontaneous formation of PDANPs within 1 min. The PDANPs prepared under the optimum conditions showed intense fluorescence (quantum yield, 6.3%) greater than that of previously reported fluorescent PDANPs, high production yield (about 21.0%), excellent water dispersibility and good photostability. Moreover, it was found that the fluorescence of PDANPs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+. After optimizing the sensing conditions, this method shows a wide linear ranged from 0.3 to 100 μM, while the detection limit is 0.1 μM. Furthermore, this fluorescent probe was successfully utilized for detecting Fe3+ in environmental water. Ultimately, the fluorescent PDANPs were further used for the preparation of fluorescent paper sensor to visually and semi-quantitatively detect Fe3+. Thus, the prepared PDANPs show great application potential for detecting Fe3+ in the fields of sensing and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: For the past decades, various fluorescent nanoparticles have been manufactured and widely utilized in different research fields, including sensing [1,2], biological imaging [3,4], drug delivery [5,6] and disease therapy [7]. Based on their composition, the fluorescent nanoparticles can be mainly divided into three types, including fluorescent proteins (FPs) [8], fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (FINs) [9] and fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) [10–12]. A fluorescent nanoparticle with excellent application potential should possess the features of facile preparation processes, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, good photostability and high water dispersibility. As is well-known, FPs typically have poor thermal stability and their preparation processes are also tedious and expensive. FINs, including carbon dots (CDs) [13], semiconductor quantum dots [14], metal nanoclusters [15] and silicon quantum dots [16], exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties and have attracted lots of attention. Nevertheless, the intrinsic drawbacks of FINs often limit their further application, such as strong toxicity, low biodegradability, tedious preparation processes and high energy consumption [17]. Thus, it is significant to further develop novel methods for synthesizing the alternative fluorescent nanoparticles which contribute to overcoming the shortcomings of FPs and FINs.

Compared with their inorganic counterparts, FONs are expected to exhibit a greater potential for some applications due to their attractive advantages such as good biodegradation, superior biocompatibility, low toxicity, easily modified structural and functional features [10,11]. For this reason, a series of strategies to synthesize FONs have been developed based on some precursors, such as small organic dyes or aggregation induced emission (AIE) dyes [18,19]. However, there are still many problems in the preparation and application of FONs. For instance, the FONs synthesized from conventional organic dyes tend to result in a significant reduction in their fluorescence intensity after their self-assembly into nanoparticles due to the well-known quenching effect caused by aggregation [20]. Although AIE-based FONs could avoid this quenching effect, their preparation processes are quite complicated and time-consuming [21]. Additionally, the commonly used AIE-active molecules are high-cost and hydrophobic, which can further limit the practical application of AIE-based FONs. Therefore, it is worthwhile to develop more facile and fast methods for synthesizing novel FONs with excellent fluorescent properties and good water dispersion stability.

Poly(dopamine) (PDA), the mussel-inspired melanin-mimicking material, has shown great potential for fabricating functionalized FONs owing to its attractive properties such as good biocompatibility, latent chemical reactivity and high affinity for different metal ions [3,10,22–24]. Moreover, PDA not only can be utilized as a biocompatible fluorescent sensor for sensing and biological imaging, but also may have significant fluorescence quenching effect on the adjacent fluorophores through possible energy transfer and/or electron transfer process [24], which further extend the potential application of its fluorescence property. Zhang's group proposed a top-down strategy utilizing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as an oxidant to synthesize the fluorescent PDA nanoparticles (PDANPs) [3]. Chu and co-workers developed a bottom-up method using MnO2 nanosheets as oxidants to produce the fluorescent PDANPs via the oxidation and polymerization of dopamine (DA) [25]. In addition, fluorescent PDANPs could also be produced via the co-polymerization of DA and polyethyleneimine (PEI), which contributed to decreasing the aggregation degree of PDA and weakening the self-quenching effects [26]. However, the PDANPs obtained by the aforementioned fabrication strategies typically possessed low quantum yields (QY, less than 1.2%) and involved the mass use of hazardous reagents (H2O2) or a tedious preparation process, which would further restrict their large-scale applications. As our group recently reported, the PDANPs with significantly enhanced intrinsic fluorescent performance could be synthesized at room temperature by a novel bottom-up strategy, which was based on the redox modulation of PDA surface chemistry [27]. Although this study paves a facile route to improve the luminescent property of PDANPs, this method still has some disadvantages such as a lengthy synthesis process (more than 36 h) as well as relatively low QY (5.1%) and poor production yield. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to exploit new strategies for rapidly synthesizing highly fluorescent PDANPs on a large scale.

On the other hand, the strategies for large-scale synthesizing fluorescent CDs at room temperature based on the self-exothermic reaction systems has captured tremendous attention in recent years [28–30]. Compared with other routes, the scalable preparation methods are more energy-efficient and concise, requiring neither external heat treatments nor complex operations. Moreover, the spontaneous formation of CDs can typically be achieved within a few minutes, which greatly shortens the synthesis time. Considering the similarities in the bottom-up routes for fabricating fluorescent CDs and PDANPs, it is an interesting possibility that the self-exothermic reaction assisted methods are also likely to be used in the scalable fabrication of PDA-derived FONs. However, there are currently no reports focusing on the self-exothermic reaction assisted room temperature synthesis of fluorescent FONs.

Herein, inspired by our previous study and the recently reported studies on the self-exothermic reaction assisted rapid synthesis of fluorescent CDs, we firstly reported a novel route for the scalable and rapid synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs at room temperature via a self-exothermic reaction assisted strategy, using DA as the sole precursor. The facile method was based on the spontaneous heating effect of the self-exothermic redox reaction system composed of the oxidant sodium periodate (SP) and the reductant sodium borohydride (NaBH4) (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared PDANPs exhibited superior fluorescence performance, good photostability and excellent water dispersibility, possessing a great potential for the rapid and visual detection of Fe3+. The study not only develops a novel green methodology for room temperature synthesizing fluorescent PDANPs, but also possesses great potential for visual sensing, clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

Sodium periodate, dopamine hydrochloride, sodium hydrosulfide (NaHS), sodium citrate (SC), sodium bisulfite (NaHSO3) and ascorbic acid (AA) were all bought from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. All the metal salts, quinine sulfate dehydrate and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were obtained from Huaxia Reagent Co., Ltd. Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, sodium borohydride, hydrochloric acid and ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA) were obtained from KeLong Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis membranes (MWCO 1000 Da) were obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water was prepared by an ultrapure water system (AWL-1002-H).

An electron microscope (JEM-1200EX) was used to observe TEM images. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 was utilized to perform the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements. A PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer was utilized to perform the fluorescence measurements, while UV–visible absorption spectra was obtained by a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600). A Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer was used to record FTIR spectra. The elemental component analysis of fluorescent PDANPs were performed by a Thermo Electron ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of fluorescent PDANPs
ce:section.ce:para: A facile procedure was designed to synthesize the fluorescent PDANPs by mixing DA, SP, NaBH4 and sodium acetate buffer (Scheme 1). The synthesis processes were optimized by adjusting the pH value of buffer and the amounts of reactants to obtain the maximum QY. Typically, DA (70 mg, 0.4 mmol) was firstly dissolved in sodium acetate solution (pH 6.0, 10 mL, 0.5 mmol) at room temperature, then slowly added SP (4.0 g, 18.7 mmol) into the DA solution. Subsequently, NaBH4 (1.0 g, 26.4 mmol) was rapidly poured into the DA/SP mixed solution in a 250 mL beaker. The solution was slightly stirred throughout the whole reaction, during which lots of reaction heat was released in a short time (CAUTION – rapid exotherm), leading to the temperature of the mixed solution increased from 24.9 to 103.2 °C within 20 s. In the meantime, the DA solution rapidly deepened to black brown within 1 min, indicating the successful formation of PDANPs. When the temperature of the obtained solution dropped to room temperature, a dialysis tube (MWCO 1000 Da) was used to dialyze the solution against ultrapure water for 48 h, then the dialyzed pale-yellow solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter and lyophilized to obtain the powder (Fig. S1). According to the weight difference of DA precursor and obtained PDANPs powder, the measured production yield of fluorescent PDANPs was approximately 21.0%. Finally, the product was dissolved to 2.5 mg/mL by ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C.

Firstly, the Fe3+ stock solution with a concentration of 50 mM was prepared by dissolving the FeCl3 in ultrapure water. Then the different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions were obtained by diluting the stock solution with ultrapure water. 200 μL of different concentration of Fe3+ solutions, 200 μL of fluorescent PDANPs solution and 100 μL of 2.5 mM EDTA solution were orderly added into a fluorescent cuvette. Subsequently, HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) was added into the solutions (the final concentration of fluorescent PDANPs: 125 μg/mL). After reaction for 3 min, the fluorescence spectra of the above incubated solutions were recorded under 350 nm excitation. To confirm the selectivity of fluorescent PDANPs for the Fe3+ detection, other metal ions were detected in a similar way. Under excitation at 350 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra at 430 nm were recorded in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the Fe3+ detection in environmental water samples, we collected several samples from the lab, Tuojiang River and Yanyu Lake (Luzhou, Sichuan, China). Firstly, the water samples from the lake and river were centrifuged at a speed of 10000 rpm for 15 min, then the insoluble substances were removed utilizing a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The sample of tap water was boiled to remove residual chlorine. The applicability of the nanosensor platform was evaluated by standard addition method. Specifically, the recovery was calculated according to the presented detection methodology by adding different concentration of Fe3+ standard solutions into the above water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the cellulose filter paper was carefully dipped into the surface of the fluorescent PDANPs stock solution for 2 min to evenly distribute the fluorescent PDANPs through capillary action. After the solution was gradually dried at room temperature, the functionalized fluorescent indicator paper was obtained. Afterwards, 100 μL different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions after the addition of EDTA (100 μL, 2.5 mM) were dropped on these paper-based sensors, respectively. Similarly, other metal ion solutions were detected to confirm the selectivity of the paper-based sensor for semi-quantitatively and visually sensing Fe3+. After incubating for 40 s at room temperature, the fluorescence changes of the tested paper-based sensors were observed by a digital camera under a 365 nm UV lamp.

As previously reported, the strong oxidant SP not only can induce the rapid oxidative polymerization of DA and the controlled growth of PDANPs, but also can effectively improve the luminescent property of the products by mitigating the fluorescence quenching effect caused by the π-π interaction between the oligomeric units in PDA [27]. Therefore, SP was chosen to be the oxidant to construct the possible self-exothermic redox reaction system for the rapid synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs. In order to form a high-efficiency self-exothermic redox reaction system, a series of combinations of reductants and oxidant SP were used as the heat sources to promote the synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs from DA at room temperature. As depicted in Fig. 1 , after mixing SP with several common reductants, including NaHS, AA, NaHSO3 and SC, the temperature of the mixed solutions increased only slightly and failed to form fluorescent PDANPs. By contrast, after mixing SP with NaBH4, the temperature was rapidly increased from 24.9 °C to 103.8 °C in 20 s by a digital thermometer. Consequently, heat generated from the redox reaction between SP and NaBH4, which was accumulated in the micro-regions of the mixed solutions, could significantly accelerate the polymerization rates of DA and promote the rapid growth of PDANPs within 1 min.

Furthermore, the influences of NaBH4 concentration, SP/NaBH4 ratio, the pH values of the buffer and the amounts of the precursor DA on the fluorescence intensity were studied. As depicted in Fig. S2A-B, with the increase of the concentration of NaBH4 and the mass ratio of SP and NaBH4, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared PDA solutions was gradually enhanced, reaching a maximum when the concentration of NaBH4 was 0.10 g/mL and their mass ratio was 4:1. The significant influences of redox reaction conditions on the fluorescent performances of PDANPs could be ascribed to the strong correlation between the surface chemistry and luminescence properties of PDA [27]. In the subsequent studies, NaBH4 and SP with the concentrations of 0.10 and 0.40 g/mL were selected for further study. On the other hand, as illustrated in Fig. S2C-D, with increasing the pH values of the reaction solutions till to 6.0 and the amounts of DA to 7.0 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity was continuously increased. Further increase in the pH values and the amounts of DA would lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensity, which could be attributed to the enhanced fluorescence quenching effects caused by the stronger π-π stacking interaction existing in the overgrown PDANPs [27]. In follow-up experiments, the buffer pH value at 6.0 and DA concentration with 7.0 mg/mL were chosen as the optimum conditions to prepare fluorescent PDANPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared fluorescent PDANPs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image shows that the fluorescent PDANPs synthesized under the optimum conditions appear as quasi-spherical particles with good mono-dispersity (Fig. 2 A). The size distribution shown in the illustration of Fig. 2A, calculated by measuring approximately 50 particles, indicates that the as-prepared PDANPs have a size distribution ranged from 22 to 42 nm, with the average diameter approximately 30 nm. Additionally, we also determined the hydrodynamic diameter of the fluorescent PDANPs by DLS measurement (Fig. S3). The result showed that the hydrodynamic diameter of fluorescent PDANPs was mainly distributed over the range from 20 to 50 nm and the average hydrodynamic diameter was approximately 29 nm, which was highly consistent with the characterization result of TEM measurement. Then, FTIR spectroscopy was applied to identify the different compositions and surface groups of PDANPs. As depicted in Fig. 2B, the FTIR spectra of PDANPs was different from DA. In curve a (DA), the absorption bands around 1612 cm−1 could be attributed to the presence of the aromatic rings [31]. After adding SP and NaBH4, the absorption bands related to DA disappeared in curve b. In addition, a series of new absorbance bands appeared in the spectrum of PDANPs around 1416, 1566, 1650 and 3420 cm−1, which were related to the stretching vibration of –COOH, C–N, CO, and –OH, respectively [32–35]. The lack of aromatic rings and appearance of new absorption bands verified the formation of PDANPs.

XPS spectra was utilized to characterize the surface functional groups and the composition of the fluorescent PDANPs. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the observed main peaks were C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. The narrow spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3B) could be correlated with several peaks of C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–O/C–N (286.8 eV), CO (288.2 eV), and C–O–C/OC–OH (290.1 eV) [36,37]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) exhibited three main peaks, whose binding energies were 531.1, 533.4 and 535.2 eV, respectively, corresponding to C–O/CO, C–OH and –COOH/O–C–O [38]. In the case of N 1s (Fig. 3D), the deconvoluted spectrum could be fitted by three distinct peaks at 399.1, 400.6 and 402.8 eV, which could be ascribed to CN, C–N and nitrogen oxide, respectively [39–41]. These results of XPS analysis are consistent with the FTIR analysis findings and the oxidant characteristics of periodate, i.e. the excess oxidant SP can rapidly cleave the oxidative o-quinone assisted by the lots of heat generated from the redox reaction system, resulting in a mass loss of aromatic rings and the appearance of carboxyl/carbonyl groups on the surface of PDANPs [42]. Moreover, it is noteworthy that the existence of CN bonds on the surface of PDANPs, which might be derive from the Schiff base condensation reaction between the oxidation products of DA, was conducive to the improvement of the fluorescence property of PDANPs because the fluorescence emitting moieties had significant effect on the strong fluorescence performance of FONs [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and photostability of fluorescent PDANPs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were utilized to investigate the optical properties of PDANPs. As exhibited in Fig. 4 A, PDANPs showed a broadband featureless absorption spectrum which approximated to that of the natural eumelanin, indicating the successful fabrication of PDA. Meanwhile, under optimal excitation wavelengths of 350 nm, the fluorescence emission spectrum showed a strong peak at 432 nm with a Stokes shift of 82 nm. From the photographs illustrated in the inset of Fig. 4A, the PDANPs solution was pale yellow and almost transparent under visible light but emitted a bright blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (365 nm). To further investigate the fluorescence behavior of PDANPs, the fluorescence spectra were determined under increasing excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4B, when exposing PDANPs to the excitation wavelength of 310–410 nm with an increment of 10 nm, the maximum emission intensity increased first and then reduced, accompanying by a red-shift. Such excitation-dependent features were similar to previous researches on fluorescent PDANPs, which could be ascribed to the heterogeneous surface states of PDANPs [44].

The photostability of a fluorescent nanoparticle is an important prerequisite for the possible practical applications. Therefore, the influences of pH, NaCl concentration, UV irradiation and long-term storage on the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs were evaluated in more detail. As showed in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs excited at 350 nm only varied slightly between pH 3 and 11, indicating that the prepared PDANPs can be used in a wide pH range. Despite possessing a good photostability under different pH conditions, the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs actually had a maximum when the pH value was 7.0. Thus, to obtain a superior fluorescence detection performance, we used the HEPES (10 mM, pH 7.0) as the neutral buffer for further study. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs basically remained unchanged with the increase of NaCl concentration (0–500 mM) (Fig. S4b), showing their good performance to resist high ionic strength. The PDANPs solution was exposed to a xenon lamp for 120 min and the corresponding fluorescence intensity almost remained constant (Fig. S4c), manifesting that they had a good resistance to light bleaching. From all the above results, it can be confirmed that the synthesized PDANPs have an exciting potential for sensing under extreme conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PDANPs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the previous reported strong fluorescence quenching phenomenon of PDANPs caused by the specific interaction between some metal ions and the functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of PDANPs [12], we further studied whether the as-prepared PDANPs could respond to metal ions selectively and sensitively. Through adding the representative metal ions, including K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Hg2+ to the solutions of PDANPs, the selectivity of the nanosensor to detect metal ions was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, apart from Fe3+, several other metal ions also had a different degree of quenching effect on PDANPs. However, after adding EDTA into PDANPs solution, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) with and without various metal ions significantly increased to a value close to that of the control group aside from Fe3+. Therefore, under the masking of EDTA, Fe3+ exhibited a highly selective quenching effect on PDANPs, which could be interpreted as Fe3+ had a stronger capacity to bind with the functional groups on the surface of PDANPs under the chelation of EDTA to other metal ions [45].

Then the co-existence selectivity of the nanoprobe for the potential interfering ions was further investigated to exclude possible cross-reactions. As exhibited in the blue bar of Fig. 5A, there were no significant changes in the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ coexisting with other metal ions, indicating that the proposed Fe3+-selective nanoprobe has good anti-interference ability. To acquire the optimum performance for detecting Fe3+, we further measured the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs before and after adding the Fe3+ at different pH values and calculated their ratios. As exhibited in Fig. S5, as the pH value increased from 3.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity ratios continuously decreased and reached its minimum at pH 7.0. Further increase in the pH value could lead to the slight increase of the fluorescence intensity ratios. Thus, the pH value of HEPES buffer was chosen as 7.0 for the subsequent Fe3+ sensing studies.

Fig. S6 shows the change in the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs after the addition of EDTA (2.5 mM, 100 μL) with increased concentration of Fe3+. It was obvious that the fluorescence of PDANPs gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 1 mM. Moreover, Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescent quenching value ΔF (ΔF = F0 – F) had a satisfying linearity (R2 = 0.9974) when the concentration of Fe3+ ranged from 0.3 to 100 μM. The detection limit for determining Fe3+ could be calculated to be approximately 0.1 μM based on formula 3σ/slope, while the concentration of Fe3+ permitted in drinking water was 5.4 μM [46]. Thus, this fluorescent PDANPs could be used to detect Fe3+ in drinking water. As Table S1 listed, this method was more sensitive to detect Fe3+ than several recently reported fluorescent nanoprobes [27,45,47–50]. Therefore, the as-prepared PDANPs show great potential to detect Fe3+ in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to PDANPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence quenching generally results from the static or dynamic quenching of the interaction between the quencher and the fluorescence molecule. Both static and dynamic quenching can be simply described via the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + K
sv [Fe3+], where K
sv is the quenching constant, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity with or without the quencher. As depicted in Fig. S7A, there was an exciting linearity between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration ranged from 0.3 to 100 μM (R2 = 0.9997) and the value of K
sv was estimated to be 1.07 × 103 M−1, demonstrating the presence of static or dynamic quenching. To further explore the quenching mechanism of PDANPs by Fe3+, based on the time-correlated single photon counting method, we performed the time resolved fluorescence decay analysis. As depicted in Fig. S7B, after adding Fe3+ into the PDANPs solution, the fluorescence lifetime of PDANPs (5.304 ns) was almost the same as that without Fe3+ (5.379 ns), which excluded the possible existence of dynamic quenching [51]. Furthermore, the quenching rate (kq) can be calculated to be 1.99 × 1011 M−1 s−1 according to the equation K
sv = kqτ0, where τ0 = 5.379 ns. The kq value was far above the reported typical diffusion-controlled quenching rate (1010 M−1 s−1), manifesting the occurrence of fast electron transfer behavior. These above results demonstrate that the static quenching occurred between PDANPs and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples and visual detection of Fe3+ by paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reliability and practicality of the developed PDANPs-based sensing system were investigated by detecting Fe3+ in environmental water samples. Fig. S8-10 showed the change in the fluorescence response of PDANPs in presence of different environmental water samples (including river, tap and lake water) containing EDTA (2.5 mM, 100 μL) and different amounts of Fe3+, respectively. Similarly, the fluorescent quenching values observed under practical conditions also had a good linear relationship to the concentration of Fe3+ within the range from 0.3 to 100 μM. Furthermore, the three environmental water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ (3, 5, and 8 μM) and the recovery test was conducted. As listed in Table S2, the obtained recoveries were distributed in 96.4–108.7% and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was lower than 5.3%, demonstrating the presented sensing system can be used effectively for the accurate detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples.

Furthermore, the visual and on-site detection of metal ions still exists a great challenge. Herein, we developed a facile, quick and low-cost paper-based detection method for visual and on-site sensing of Fe3+. By dipping the cellulose filter paper into the surface of the PDANPs solutions, the Fe3+-selective paper-based sensor could be fabricated easily. In order to obtain the optimum visual sensing performance, the influence of incubating time on the visual detection effect for Fe3+ was detailedly investigated. As depicted in Fig. S11, the fluorescence of PDANPs-doped filter papers was weakened continuously after dropping 500 μM Fe3+ solution within 40 s and hardly changed if the incubating time was further increased. Therefore, the incubating time of 40 s was selected for the following study.

Different metal ion solutions with the concentration of 500 μM were dripped onto the as-prepared paper-based sensors to evaluate the selectivity. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, under the masking of EDTA, the addition of other metal ions would not cause significant changes in the color of the PDANPs-doped filter papers except for Fe3+, which made it convenient to distinguish Fe3+ from other metal ions. In addition, the solutions containing different concentrations of Fe3+ were dripped onto the as-prepared PDANPs-doped filter papers to test the semi-quantitative performance of the paper-based sensor. As shown in Fig. 6B, the test paper emitted a bright blue fluorescence in the absence of Fe3+. With the increase of Fe3+ concentration from 5 to 500 μM, the color of the test paper gradually darkened, which was visually discernible by eye. Therefore, the PDANPs functionalized paper-based sensor has a great potential for the semi-quantitative and visual detection of Fe3+ due to its easy-operation, low-cost and good portability.

In conclusion, we have designed a rapid, facile and green method for large-scale synthesizing fluorescent PDANPs at room temperature, utilizing easily available DA as the precursor, the self-exothermic redox reaction system composed of SP and NaBH4 as the accelerant and regulator for the growth of PDANPs. Compared with previously reported fabrication strategies of PDA-based FONs, the presented self-exothermic reaction assisted strategy is faster, more energy-efficient and easy to expand production. The as-prepared PDANPs exhibited superior fluorescence performance, good photostability and excellent water dispersibility, possessing a great potential for practical applications. Furthermore, the fluorescent PDANPs had been successfully used as a fluorescent nanoprobe to detect Fe3+ sensitively and selectively in environmental water samples. More intriguingly, the fluorescent PDANPs could be readily utilized for the fabrication of fluorescent paper sensor to semi-quantitatively and visually detect Fe3+. Overall, the present work not only provides a novel method and effective way to synthesize FONs, but also has broad application prospects in the fields of sensing, clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

Yuanhua Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Software. Yan Qiu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kai Zhou: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kailian Zhang: Data curation. Lujun Wang: Writing - review & editing. Jing Zeng: Writing - review & editing. Baian Ji: Validation, Visualization. Die Gao: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Zhining Xia: Validation, Visualization. Qifeng Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - reviewing & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21804113 and 21974015), the Science and Technology Strategic Cooperation Project of Luzhou Municipal People's Government and Southwest Medical University (Grant No. 2019LZXNYDC03), the Talent Introduction Project of Southwest Medical University (Grant No. 090300040005) and the Science and Technology Transformation Project of Southwest Medical University (Grant No. 2018001).
